era_context: The early Iron Age. Shalmaneser III's reign in Assyria is well-established. Greece is in the Geometric period. Egypt is fragmented. China's Western Zhou is in decline, approaching the Spring and Autumn period. The Mediterranean is dominated by Phoenician trade and emerging Greek commerce.

events.title: Shalmaneser III's military campaigns and tributary system
events.description: Shalmaneser III continues his military expansion and consolidation of the tributary system in the Levant and Syria. His campaigns establish Assyrian hegemony over the region and create a network of vassal states that will characterize Near Eastern politics for the next century. The campaigns are documented in Assyrian royal inscriptions and represent the military-administrative apparatus of the Neo-Assyrian Empire.
events.certainty_note: Shalmaneser III's reign is well-documented in royal inscriptions. The specific dating of individual campaigns within his reign has some uncertainty (±2–3 years), but the broader pattern of military expansion is secure.

events.title: Geometric pottery production and trade in the Aegean
events.description: Geometric pottery continues to be produced and traded throughout the Aegean, indicating growing economic interconnection among Greek communities. Pottery from Corinth and Athens is distributed widely, suggesting the emergence of regional trade networks. This period marks the gradual recovery of the Greek world from the Bronze Age collapse.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from pottery distribution studies

events.certainty_note: Pottery distribution and typology are well-established through archaeological survey and excavation. Dating within the Geometric period is approximate (±10–15 years).

disconfirming_evidence: No disconfirming evidence identified for events listed.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 779 BCE is sparse and regionally concentrated. The Near East (Assyria) has the best documentation through royal inscriptions. Greece has archaeological evidence but no contemporary written records. Most of the world has no written documentation for this year. The surviving sources are heavily biased toward imperial narratives (Assyrian inscriptions) and material culture (pottery). The historical record is dominated by military and economic narratives from elite perspectives.

graph_edges.note: Shalmaneser III's campaigns in 779 BCE are part of his broader military program
